title: City Point (Brooklyn)

infobox.value: 1 City Point 10 City Point City Tower One DeKalb Avenue
2015 (City Point I)  2016 (City Point II) 2020 (City Point III)
361 feet (110 m) (City Point I) 525 feet (160 m) (City Point II) 720 feet (220 m) (City Point III)
1.6 to 1.9 million square feet (150,000 to 180,000 m2)

is a mixed-use multi-building residential and commercial complex in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[1]](#cite_note-1) City Point is, by square footage, the largest mixed-use development in the city. City Point III, standing at 720 feet in height, is currently the second [tallest building in Brooklyn](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brooklyn "List of tallest buildings in Brooklyn") (behind the [Brooklyn Tower](/wiki/Brooklyn_Tower "Brooklyn Tower")) as well as the fourth tallest on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") (behind the Brooklyn Tower, the [Skyline Tower](/wiki/Skyline_Tower_(Queens) "Skyline Tower (Queens)") in [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City "Long Island City"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Sven](/wiki/Sven_(development) "Sven (development)") in Long Island City).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

and was being designed by the firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox "Kohn Pedersen Fox").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This would be the only for-sale residential development at City Point.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Donnelly, Tim (September 6, 2017). ["The crazy real estate boom taking over Flatbush Avenue"](https://nypost.com/2017/09/06/the-crazy-real-estate-boom-taking-over-flatbush-avenue/). *New York Post*. Retrieved September 12, 2017.

Rinn, Natalie (April 28, 2016). ["Inside City Tower, the Future of Downtown Brooklyn"](http://www.bkmag.com/2016/04/28/tour-city-tower-new-downtown-brooklyn/). *Brooklyn Magazine*. Retrieved September 12, 2017.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mixed-use development in Brooklyn, New York

**City Point** is a mixed-use multi-building residential and commercial complex in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[1]](#cite_note-1) City Point is, by square footage, the largest mixed-use development in the city. City Point III, standing at 720 feet in height, is currently the second [tallest building in Brooklyn](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brooklyn "List of tallest buildings in Brooklyn") (behind the [Brooklyn Tower](/wiki/Brooklyn_Tower "Brooklyn Tower")) as well as the fourth tallest on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") (behind the Brooklyn Tower, the [Skyline Tower](/wiki/Skyline_Tower_(Queens) "Skyline Tower (Queens)") in [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City "Long Island City"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and [Sven](/wiki/Sven_(development) "Sven (development)") in Long Island City).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

City Point was supported by the [New York City Economic Development Corporation](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation") as a sustainable mixed-use development for retail and housing.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3) The project was developed by Albee Development LLC and designed by Cook + Fox architects, and is [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design")-silver certified.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was expected to create at least 328 construction jobs and 108 permanent jobs.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The complex is built over an entrance to the [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_(BMT_Fourth_Avenue_Line) "DeKalb Avenue (BMT Fourth Avenue Line)") station on the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")'s [B](/wiki/B_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "B (New York City Subway service)")​, ​[Q](/wiki/Q_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "Q (New York City Subway service)")​, and ​[R](/wiki/R_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "R (New York City Subway service)") trains. It is across the [Flatbush Avenue Extension](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") from [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University")'s [Brooklyn campus](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn "LIU Brooklyn"), and across Fleet Street from the Brooklyn Tower. City Point is located on the former site of the [Albee Square Mall](/wiki/Albee_Square_Mall "Albee Square Mall"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) and its southern entrance is centered on the [Fulton Street Mall](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Brooklyn)#Fulton_Mall "Fulton Street (Brooklyn)").

## Description and history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Point_(Brooklyn)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description and history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/City_Point_Tower_II_Brooklyn_NY_2015_06_10_08.jpg/250px-City_Point_Tower_II_Brooklyn_NY_2015_06_10_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_Point_Tower_II_Brooklyn_NY_2015_06_10_08.jpg)

Looking southeast along [Flatbush Avenue Extension](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue_Extension "Flatbush Avenue Extension") in 2015 at City Point Tower II on the left and City Point Tower I on the right. The site of City Point Tower III, completed in 2020, is in the foreground.

In 2004, the [New York City Economic Development Corporation](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation") adopted the "Downtown Brooklyn Plan", which consisted of a series of zoning changes and public works.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3) City Point was one of the winning developments proposed, sitting on municipal-owned land, in an area already well-established as a shopping corridor.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3)

The development was stalled in permitting but was helped through by then-Brooklyn [Borough President](/wiki/Borough_President "Borough President") [Marty Markowitz](/wiki/Marty_Markowitz "Marty Markowitz"), when the developers, Acadia Realty, made donations to a non-profit Markowitz operated.[[7]](#cite_note-Markowitz-7) Markowitz and Acadia denied wrongdoing and cast the blame on the insistence of a partner firm, PA Associates, who were later indicted with bribing former [New York State Senator](/wiki/New_York_State_Senator "New York State Senator") [Carl Kruger](/wiki/Carl_Kruger "Carl Kruger").[[7]](#cite_note-Markowitz-7)

### Towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Point_(Brooklyn)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Towers")]

The first tower, City Point Tower I (also known as **7 DeKalb**),[[8]](#cite_note-8) opened in 2015. It is a 19-story, 225,000-square-foot tower with 200 units of affordable housing,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and 50,000 square feet (4,600 m2) of retail space.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3)

The second tower, City Point Tower II (also known as 1 DeKalb Avenue),[[10]](#cite_note-SkyscraperCenter15744-10) or 10 City Point, [doing business as](/wiki/Doing_business_as "Doing business as") **City Tower**[[11]](#cite_note-11) was completed in 2015[[12]](#cite_note-12) and opened in 2016. It is a 30-story, 335,000-square-foot tower with 440 market-rate units.[[3]](#cite_note-nycedc.com-3)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Andrews_classic_roadside_hamburgers_in_Dekalb_Hall_jeh.jpg/250px-Andrews_classic_roadside_hamburgers_in_Dekalb_Hall_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrews_classic_roadside_hamburgers_in_Dekalb_Hall_jeh.jpg)

Burger stand in DeKalb Hall

A third tower—City Point Tower III, located at 138 Willoughby Street planned to be 720 feet (220 m) tall, making it the tallest in Brooklyn in 2020.[[14]](#cite_note-curbed.com-14) [9 DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/9_DeKalb_Avenue "9 DeKalb Avenue") (now The Brooklyn Tower) surpassed City Point Tower III in height in 2021.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It was planned to contain 458 market-rate condo units taking up 1,082,218 square feet (100,541.3 m2), with three stories of commercial space occupying 502,460 square feet (46,680 m2).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Tower III will be doing business as **Brooklyn Point** and was being designed by the firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox "Kohn Pedersen Fox").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This would be the only for-sale residential development at City Point.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Point_(Brooklyn)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Shopping")]

Accessible by entrances on [Flatbush Avenue Extension](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue_Extension "Flatbush Avenue Extension") and on [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Brooklyn) "Fulton Street (Brooklyn)") is a shopping plaza with big box national chain stores, smaller retail shops, a movie theater, bar, and grocery store, as well as restaurants and a 27,000 square foot[[20]](#cite_note-20) food court in the basement of Tower II called DeKalb Market Hall.[[21]](#cite_note-DMH-21) DeKalb Market Hall has 40 different vendors,[[22]](#cite_note-22) small businesses based in the New York City area.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Between the first and second towers is "the podium", within which was built 660,000 square feet (61,000 m2) of retail space, including a 4-floor [Primark](/wiki/Primark "Primark") store.[[24]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Eagle_2022-24) and an [Alamo Drafthouse](/wiki/Alamo_Drafthouse "Alamo Drafthouse").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) On January 29, 2017, [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") opened its store in City Point Tower II,[[27]](#cite_note-27) with a [Trader Joe's](/wiki/Trader_Joe%27s "Trader Joe's") opening in June of the same year.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

A [Fogo de Chão](/wiki/Fogo_de_Ch%C3%A3o "Fogo de Chão") restaurant opened on the ground level in April 2024.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Statue of Ruth Bader Ginsburg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=City_Point_(Brooklyn)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Statue of Ruth Bader Ginsburg")]

The [statue of Ruth Bader Ginsburg](/wiki/Statue_of_Ruth_Bader_Ginsburg "Statue of Ruth Bader Ginsburg") was installed outside 445 [Albee Square](/wiki/Albee_Square "Albee Square") in City Point on March 12, 2021.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The statue consists of a 6 ft (1.8 m) [bronze](/wiki/Bronze_sculpture "Bronze sculpture") statue, set on a 1 ft (0.30 m) base, depicting [Ruth Bader Ginsburg](/wiki/Ruth_Bader_Ginsburg "Ruth Bader Ginsburg"), the second woman to serve on the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court").[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The statue was moved in October 2022 and relocated to [South Brooklyn Health](/wiki/South_Brooklyn_Health "South Brooklyn Health")'s Ruth Bader Ginsburg Hospital, which opened on May 2, 2023.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)
